name: Reinforcement-Learning Temporal-Difference Model with Opponent-Process Reward Shaping
description: Model-free Q-learning agent whose internal reward signal is shaped by opponent-process dynamics and an allostatic baseline. The agent learns state-action values (Q-values) via TD(0), but the reward it experiences is not the raw environment reward — it is transformed through a hedonic trace (a-process), an opponent trace (b-process), and an allostatic baseline theta that degrades with chronic intake. Early in training, eating yields positive shaped reward (positive reinforcement). As allostatic load accumulates, abstinence produces negative shaped reward (aversive) while eating provides relief (negative reinforcement), causing the learned policy to shift from pleasure-seeking to compulsion-relieving. Exploration rate is modulated by dysphoria, capturing motivational narrowing. Based on Koob & Le Moal (2001, 2008) allostatic framework applied to a Q-learning architecture inspired by Keramati & Gutkin (2014) homeostatic RL.

variables.name: q_table
variables.description: Action-value function mapping (state, action) pairs to expected shaped reward. State is a tuple of (agent_x, agent_y, discretised_h, discretised_o, food_at_position_flag).

variables.name: hedonic_trace
variables.description: Decaying trace of recent food rewards; a-process proxy

variables.name: opponent_trace
variables.description: Decaying aversive accumulator; b-process proxy that grows with sensitised gain

variables.name: allostatic_baseline
variables.description: Shifting reference for internal reward; degrades with chronic intake, making abstinence aversive

variables.name: shaped_internal_reward
variables.description: Opponent-process-modulated reward signal the agent actually experiences: r_tilde = (r_ext + h - o) - theta

variables.name: external_reward
variables.description: Raw environment reward: 1.0 if ate food, 0.0 otherwise

variables.name: cumulative_eating_count
variables.description: Total food consumption episodes to date; drives opponent gain sensitisation

variables.name: opponent_gain
variables.description: Current opponent coupling gain, grows with N (sensitisation)

variables.name: dysphoria
variables.description: Reward deficit: D = max(0, theta - (h - o)); modulates exploration rate

variables.name: exploration_rate
variables.description: Current epsilon for epsilon-greedy policy; decays with eating count and narrows with dysphoria

parameters.name: gain_growth_per_episode
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2001): b-process sensitisation with repetition

parameters.name: allostatic_drift_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): rate of baseline degradation

parameters.name: set_point_recovery_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): slow, incomplete recovery

parameters.name: initial_hedonic_baseline
parameters.source: Normalised zero-baseline for RL shaped reward

parameters.name: stress_loading_factor
parameters.source: Yau & Potenza (2013): stress accelerates allostatic degradation

parameters.name: hedonic_discretisation_bins
parameters.source: For tractable tabular RL state representation

parameters.name: opponent_discretisation_bins
parameters.source: For tractable tabular RL state representation

rules.description: Hedonic trace update: decays toward zero, boosted by external food reward
Opponent trace update: decays slowly, recruited proportionally to hedonic trace via sensitising gain
Opponent gain sensitisation: coupling gain grows linearly with cumulative eating count
Allostatic baseline drift: theta degrades proportionally to opponent trace, with very slow recovery
rules.pseudocode: theta = theta - lam * o + rho * (theta_0 - theta)

rules.description: Shaped internal reward: what the agent 'experiences' as reward, combining raw reward, affective traces, and allostatic baseline
Q-learning TD(0) update using shaped reward
rules.pseudocode:
td_error = r_tilde + gamma_rl * max(Q[s_new][a'] for a' in valid_actions(s_new)) - Q[s_old][a_old]
Q[s_old][a_old] = Q[s_old][a_old] + alpha_Q * td_error
rules.source_postulate: P4 (learning driven by allostatic reward landscape)

rules.description: Dysphoria-modulated exploration rate: epsilon decays with experience and narrows with dysphoria (motivational narrowing)
rules.pseudocode:
D = max(0.0, theta - (h - o))
epsilon = epsilon_0 * exp(-0.01 * N) * (1.0 / (1.0 + abs(D)))

rules.description: State discretisation: continuous h and o are binned for tabular Q-table indexing
rules.pseudocode:
h_bin = clip(int(h * h_bins / 3.0), 0, h_bins - 1)  # scale 0..3 into bins
o_bin = clip(int(o * o_bins / 3.0), 0, o_bins - 1)
food_flag = 1 if food_at_current_position else 0
state = (agent_x, agent_y, h_bin, o_bin, food_flag)

rules.description: Eating count increment on successful consumption
rules.pseudocode:
if action_was_eat and consumed:
    N = N + 1

decision_logic.description: Epsilon-greedy action selection over Q-values. With probability epsilon (which decreases with experience and dysphoria), the agent chooses a random valid action. Otherwise it chooses the action with highest Q-value. The 'eat' action is only valid when food is at the agent's current position. The Q-values themselves are learned from shaped internal reward (R5), so the policy naturally transitions from hedonic eating to compulsive eating as the reward landscape shifts.
pseudocode: food_at_pos = any(f for f in perception['resources']['food'] if f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'])
# Get Q-values for current state; default 0 for unseen state-action pairs
q_values = {a: Q.get((state, a), 0.0) for a in valid_actions}

perception_to_variables.position: (perception['x'], perception['y']) — used as part of state tuple
perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception['resources']['food'] — used to determine food_flag in state and valid actions
perception_to_variables.food_at_position: any food in perception['resources']['food'] with matching (x, y) → food_flag
perception_to_variables.reward_signal: reward from update(action, reward, new_perception) → r_ext (1.0 from ConsumeEffect, 0.0 otherwise)

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect with raw reward 1.0 → r_ext; this is then transformed into shaped reward r_tilde via rule R5

expected_behaviors.description: Reward shaping transition: early shaped rewards for eating are positive (pleasure); later shaped rewards for NOT eating become negative (aversive withdrawal)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: track r_tilde when action != eat over 200 steps → assert r_tilde becomes increasingly negative in later steps

expected_behaviors.description: Policy shift from exploration to exploitation of food: Q-values for eating and moving toward food increase relative to stay/random movement
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare average Q(state, 'eat') at step 50 vs step 200 relative to Q(state, 'stay') → assert food actions increasingly preferred

expected_behaviors.description: Exploration rate collapse: epsilon shrinks as N grows and D increases, modelling motivational narrowing
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: track epsilon over 200 steps → assert epsilon at step 200 < epsilon at step 1

expected_behaviors.description: Allostatic baseline degradation: theta drifts below theta_0 with chronic eating
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 200 steps with food → assert theta < theta_0 at end

expected_behaviors.description: Compulsive food-seeking emerges: in late episodes, the agent moves toward food even when h > 0, because shaped reward during abstinence is negative
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: in last 50 steps, count proportion of move actions directed toward food → assert > 80%

expected_behaviors.description: Opponent gain sensitisation: g increases monotonically with eating episodes
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: track g over time → assert g at step 200 > g at step 0 proportional to N

references: Solomon, R.L. & Corbit, J.D. (1974). An opponent-process theory of motivation: I. Temporal dynamics of affect. Psychological Review, 81(2), 119–145.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2001). Drug addiction, dysregulation of reward, and allostasis. Neuropsychopharmacology, 24(2), 97–129.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2008). Neurobiological mechanisms for opponent motivational processes in addiction. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 363(1507), 3113–3123.
Yau, Y.H.C. & Potenza, M.N. (2013). Stress and eating behaviors. Minerva Endocrinologica, 38(3), 255–267.
Keramati, M. & Gutkin, B. (2014). Homeostatic reinforcement learning for integrating reward collection and physiological stability. eLife, 3, e04811.
